Sen. Ben Sasse, R-Neb., a frequent critic of Trump, called the move “weak.”

“Putin is not our friend and he is not the President's buddy," Sasse said in a statement. "He is a thug using Soviet-style aggression to wage a shadow war against America, and our leaders should act like it.”

Democratic Senate Leader Chuck Schumer, D-N.Y., accused Trump of "turning our foreign policy into an international joke."

Trump's remarks were the latest show of disunity between the U.S. and the other six nations meeting at a resort on the Saint Lawrence River this weekend. Trump was already expected to face tense meetings following his decision to impose steep tariffs on steel and aluminum from those countries.

The meeting is getting under way a day after the president engaged in a Twitter spat with summit participants and announced he would leave the gathering before it is over. The U.S. is meeting with Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan and the United Kingdom at the summit.

“Looking forward to straightening out unfair Trade Deals with the G-7 countries,” Trump posted on Twitter early Friday. “If it doesn’t happen, we come out even better!”

On Thursday, French President Emmanuel Macron suggested the group could move forward on agreements without the United States, and Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said “American jobs are on the line because of his actions.” Both countries have joined others in threatening retaliatory tariffs.

In response, Trump posted on Twitter that Trudeau was “being so indignant” and said that both leaders were failing to make note of higher trade barriers they have leveled against U.S.-made products.

“Canada charges the U.S. a 270% tariff on Dairy Products!” the president tweeted Friday. “They didn’t tell you that, did they? Not fair to our farmers!”

The White House said late Thursday that the president would leave the summit on Saturday morning.

By doing so, Trump will skip meetings on climate change, energy policy and oceans. He’ll leave Canada to head to Singapore, where he's scheduled to meet with North Korean leader Kim Jong Un for a historic summit next week.

Trump returned to his concerns about global trade as he left the White House on Friday, saying the U.S. would terminate the North American Free Trade Agreement if it is unable to negotiate a better deal.

"If we’re unable to make a deal, we’re better off," Trump said. "I think we’ll probably very easily make a deal. All of these countries have taken advantage of the United States on trade."

Little of that tension was on display when the cameras entered the rooms of the summit, however. During a meeting Friday evening with Trudeau, Trump kidded that the Canadian leader had "agreed to cut all tariffs." Trudeau responded by saying the North American Free Trade Agreement was “in good shape,” despite the fact that talks around renegotiating that deal have largely stalled.

The president reiterated the idea that a trade agreement could take a different shape, namely, individual deals with Canada and Mexico.

"Our relationship is very good,” Trump said. “We're actually working on cutting tariffs and making it all very fair for both countries. And we’ve made a lot of progress today."

search_result.title: G7 leaders work to 'starve' Russia of oil money as Zelensky says he ...
search_result.text:
Telfs-Buchen, Austria —

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky told G7 leaders during a virtual meeting Monday that he wants the war in Ukraine to end by the end of 2022, according to a source familiar with his remarks, as leaders worked to starve Russia of its oil revenues and mitigate the economic fallout of the conflict.

The dual missions – sustain support for Ukraine while easing economic pressures at home – have defined this year’s summit of leading democracies, held high in the Bavarian Alps. While largely united, the leaders are all facing their own political imperatives to see a momentum shift in the fighting and bring an end to the conflict quickly.

To that end, leaders were nearing an agreement to try capping the price of Russian oil, depriving the country of revenues that have soared amid the global spike in energy prices. Details were still being ironed out, including whether the top buyers of Russian oil would agree to the limits, but US officials sounded confident in the prospects of the plan.

As the war in Ukraine grinds ahead, the question of how to end the conflict has loomed over the talks here. Zelensky’s timeframe, delivered via video conference during a morning session at the Schloss Elmau castle, was as clear a sign as he has given about where he sees the trajectory of the war headed.

A top adviser to President Joe Biden said Zelensky was focused on regaining momentum in Ukraine over the coming months – and not years – when he spoke to leaders.

“Zelensky was very much focused on trying to ensure that Ukraine is in as advantageous a position on the battlefield as possible in the next months as opposed to the next years, because he believes that a grinding conflict is not in the interest of the Ukrainian people,” US national security adviser Jake Sullivan said after the meeting.

The Ukraine conflict has been at the center of the Group of 7 summit being held inside a century-old mountainside castle in Germany’s Bavaria region. Leaders have decided on new steps to isolate Russia’s economy, including a ban on new imports of Russian gold, and unveiled new sanctions on Monday.

In a joint statement after their meeting with Zelensky, the leaders pledged to continue supporting Ukraine “for as long as it takes.”

The United States is expected to announce as soon as this week it’s purchased a new advanced medium-to-long range missile defense system that Ukraine has been requesting, along with new shipments of ammunition and radar systems, as it works to turn the momentum in the war away from Russia, which has been making small gains in the East.

Ukraine had requested the system to help sustain its fight against Russia. In a brief glimpse of the leaders’ meeting with Zelensky, the Ukrainian President could be seen on a screen set up in the conference room as the leaders took their seats.

G7 leaders also plan to announce a lengthy set of new sanctions, including on Russian defense supply chains, Russians responsible for human rights abuses and war crimes, private military companies and new visa restrictions on 500 officials. The US will also announce $7.5 billion in new funding for Ukraine, part of a broader commitment from G7 nations to help the country make up its budgetary shortfalls.

“Here at this meeting of the G7, as well as at NATO, we will continue to do, collectively, everything we can to make sure that the Ukrainians have what they need in their hands to repel the Russian aggression,” US Secretary of State Antony Blinken said in an exclusive interview with CNN’s “State of the Union” on Sunday.

There are other important topics on the agenda, including a new effort to counter China’s infrastructure investments in the developing world that have extended Beijing’s influence across the globe.

But how much longer the Western front can remain united against Russia is the question looming over these talks. The rising cost of energy, fears of global food shortages and the certain inevitability that war fatigue will set in have lent urgency to the discussions about where the conflict goes next.

Meeting his host, German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, on Sunday, Biden sought to underscore the importance of sticking together.

“Putin has been counting on, from the beginning, that somehow NATO and the G7 would splinter,” he said. “But we haven’t, and we’re not going to.”

Zelensky, who is also planning to address this week’s NATO summit in Madrid, has pressed the West for accelerated sanctions on Moscow and heavy artillery to beat back the Russian invaders.

His entreaties will become more urgent following Sunday’s missile hits on two residential buildings in Kyiv, the Ukrainian capital that had enjoyed relative calm in recent weeks as the fighting moved eastward. Biden condemned the attack as “barbarism.”

Yet how much further leaders will be willing to go in applying new sanctions on Russia remains to be seen. High oil prices mean Russia is making more revenue from its energy exports, despite bans in Europe and the United States.

Western leaders gathering at the G7 have decided to try capping the price of Russian oil, officials say, the latest step toward punishing Moscow while attempting to mitigate the economic effects of the war in Ukraine. How, when and by how much the price of Russian oil will be capped remains to be seen. Officials said the precise mechanism for accomplishing the cap was still being worked out.

How, when and by how much the price of Russian oil will be capped remains to be seen. Officials said the precise mechanism for accomplishing the cap was still being worked out.

But leaders will instruct their teams to work urgently toward finding a way to limit the price at which Russia can sell its oil, depriving Moscow of a key revenue source.

“The goal here is to starve Russia, starve Putin of his main source of cash and force down the price of Russian oil to help blunt the impact of Putin’s war at the pump,” said a senior US administration official.

As oil prices have skyrocketed, Russia’s oil revenues are actually up, despite global import bans. Leaders want to use their collective leverage to cut the revenue Russia receives from the countries still purchasing its oil.

How, exactly, that is done remains to be seen, though the official suggested the G7 nations have leverage through oil transportation networks that could help toward applying the cap.

High gas prices for US and European consumers are putting pressure on leaders to find ways to ease the pain.

Speaking to CNN’s Jake Tapper on “State of the Union” hours after the Russian missiles hit Kyiv, British Prime Minister Boris Johnson urged Americans, Britons and others in the West to maintain resolve in punishing Moscow, despite the effect the war has had on global oil prices.

“I would just say to people in the United States that this is something that America historically does and has to do, and that is to step up for peace and freedom and democracy,” Johnson said. “And if we let Putin get away with it, and just annex, conquer sizable parts of a free, independent, sovereign country, which is what he is poised to do … then the consequences for the world are absolutely catastrophic.”

Putin, whose country was ejected from the then-G8 in 2014 after Russia’s annexation of the Ukrainian territory of Crimea, was the subject of light mockery as leaders sat down to a working lunch Sunday.

Johnson, the last leader to arrive to the round meeting table, asked whether he should keep his suit jacket on.

“Jackets on?” he asked, before joking about how the leaders had to look tough during their talks.

“We have to show that we’re tougher than Putin,” he said.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau made a suggestion: “Barechested horseback ride,” he said, as the leaders chuckled.

search_result.title: Biden steps into G20 aiming to unite leaders in opposition to ... - CNN
search_result.text:
Bali, Indonesia —

President Joe Biden is confronting competing issues at home and abroad while he’s at the Group of 20 Summit in Bali this week, using the moment on the world’s stage to lean into international support for condemning Russia’s aggression while also facing the prospect of hearing Donald Trump announce his next run for the presidency.

Administration officials previewing Biden’s G20 summit activities had their sights set on the coalition’s efforts to voice its opposition against the war in Ukraine, which could send a powerful signal amongst a group that’s so far had fragmented approaches to the Kremlin’s aggression.

This marks the first time the group has gathered in-person since the start of the invasion, and most G20 members are expected to sign onto a statement condemning Russia’s war in Ukraine “and the human suffering it has caused both for Ukrainians and for families in the developing world that are facing food and fuel insecurity as a result,” a senior administration official said.

Such an expression of condemnation has been the work of months of diplomacy between G20 leaders. However, it’s not clear yet exactly which countries will sign onto the declaration.

Although the G20 is comprised of world powers who have long backed Ukraine during the war, it also includes other nations that have been tepid in their response to Russia’s aggression – including India, China, Saudi Arabia and Indonesia, the host of this year’s summit. The coalition, which is broadly focused on the global economy, also includes Russia itself. But Russian President Vladimir Putin is not making an appearance at the summit this year.

Since the spring, US officials have anticipated a showdown at this year’s G20 over the war. Biden has stated Russia should no longer be a member of the bloc, though expelling Moscow would require support from all of the G20’s members.

As of now, no official “family photo” is listed on a schedule, a sign of the deep acrimony within the G20 spurred by the war in Ukraine.

The president’s diplomatic Tuesday – a day working alongside leaders, including a meeting with Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan – is expected to precede a 2024 presidential campaign announcement by Biden’s predecessor, Trump, from the other side of the world. The prospective announcement would set the stage for a two-year battle for the American presidency, having the power to cast a shadow over Biden’s efforts to unify world leaders – some already personally stung by Trump’s nationalist approach.

Biden and his team have already spent time during his multi-leg, cross-continental trip abroad addressing domestic politics, suggesting the issue has not only loomed on their minds, but also among their foreign counterparts in meetings throughout their travels.

On Sunday, US national security adviser Jake Sullivan told reporters that “many leaders” at the ASEAN Summit addressed the midterms with Biden, that many leaders were “following them closely” and that the president now feels he has a strong position on the international stage.

Vote counts for midterm races last Tuesday continue to trickle in, with Democrats only securing their continued majority in the US Senate this past weekend and the future of the US House of Representatives remaining up in the air. But Biden – who has frequently cast the US’ dynamic with other world powers as a global fight between democracy and autocracy – brought up the political headwinds working in his favor on Monday in Bali after he took part in a roughly three-hour meeting with Xi Jinping.

At a news conference after his meeting with Xi, Biden sought to cast the election results seen so far as a victory for the future of American democracy – a matter he had said was at stake at the polls.

“The American people proved once again that democracy is who we are. There was a strong rejection of election deniers at every level from those seeking to lead our states and those seeking to serve in Congress and also those seeking to oversee the elections,” Biden said at the start of his remarks after the Xi meeting.

The president also met with Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy. The meeting with Meloni was Biden’s first chance to confer with the new Italian prime minister in person since she took office in October – when she became the country’s most far-right leader since Benito Mussolini.

In a readout, the White House said the pair discussed a range of issues, but most notably the war in Ukraine.

“The two leaders also discussed their commitment to continue providing Ukraine the support it needs to defend itself and to holding Russia accountable for its aggression,” a US readout said.

On Tuesday, Biden participated in working sessions and a luncheon with leaders at the summit. He also co-hosted an event on the Partnership for Global Infrastructure and Investment, which the White House said “aims to mobilize $600 billion in the next five years with G7 partners to deliver sustainable infrastructure and advance U.S. national security and economic security interests.”

Biden spoke about a range of investments by the US and multilateral institutions around the world at the event, saying that “this is something that we have an obligation to do.”

Biden ticked through investments announced previously by a group formed by the Group of 7 nations, widely seen as a counter to China’s Belt and Road Initiative. “I want to emphasize the word ‘investments.’ Investments that are driven by local needs,” Biden said.

Biden then announced the Just Energy Transition Partnership with Indonesia (the White House has released a fact sheet), which seeks to mobilize $20 billion in public and private money “to support Indonesia’s efforts to reduce emissions” and transition away from fossil fuels to renewable energy.

The global infrastructure initiative event follows a launch in 2021 amongst G7 partners to better position the US and its allies to compete with China. China’s Belt and Road Initiative, first announced in 2013 under Xi, aims to build ports, roads and railways to create new trade corridors linking China to Africa and the rest of Eurasia. The Chinese-funded, cross-continental infrastructure initiative has been seen as an extension of the country’s sharp ascent to global power.

Other topics at the summit, the senior administration official said, include economic coordination, climate change, and the Covid-19 pandemic, with new announcements expected on digital infrastructure in the Indo-Pacific and solar power in Honduras.

Biden ended up skipping a leaders dinner on Tuesday to attend to other matters, an administration official said.

“It’s not Covid,” the official made clear. Biden had tested negative earlier Tuesday after coming into contact with the Cambodian prime minister, who tested positive for the virus.

“He just had spent a full day in meetings and needed to attend to a few things tonight (nothing urgent!),” the official said. Biden spoke to Indonesian President Joko Widodo to send his regrets for not attending the dinner.
authors: Maegan Vazquez Kevin Liptak Betsy Klein

search_result.title: G-7 summit, Netanyahu out, Djokovic wins: Weekend's biggest news
search_result.text:
Editors

USA TODAY

Updated June 13, 2021, 5:42 p.m. ET

COVID-19 probe in China, 15% tax: Takeaways from the G-7 summit

The Group of Seven summit in Cornwall, England, ended Sunday with world leaders calling for collective action to end the COVID-19 pandemic, reinvigorate global economies and take swift action to protect an imperiled planet from climate change. Here's what we learned:

The G-7's communiqué called for a "timely, transparent, expert-led and science-based WHO-convened" study into COVID-19's origins in China.

Leaders committed to the "Carbis Bay Declaration," a series of recommendations and steps their countries will take to prevent future pandemics.

Low coal is the goal: The countries will, by the end of this year, halt all government support for international thermal coal power generation.

And leaders endorsed a U.S. plan for a minimum global corporate tax rate of 15%

Up next on the agenda for Biden is a trip to Brussels for a NATO alliance summit on Monday, then separate talks with European Union leaders on Tuesday. But first, he stopped at Windsor Castle for tea with the queen.

Israeli lawmakers vote out Netanyahu; new prime minister named

Israel's parliament — the 120-member Knesset — voted to swear in a new government Sunday, ending the historic 12-year rule of Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. Far-right politician Naftali Bennett, who once worked for Netanyahu, becomes Israel's new prime minister for two years in a coalition agreement that includes eight separate parties and is led by Bennett and centrist Yair Lapid. Lapid will serve as foreign minister and become prime minister after Bennett's two-year stint. The move relegates Netanyahu, Israel's longest serving prime minister who is sometimes known as "King Bibi," to an opposition figure and increases his legal jeopardy as he battles corruption charges in an ongoing criminal trial. In his speech to parliament, Netanyahu made clear he has no plans on giving up leadership of the Likud Party, vowing to “continue the great mission of my life, ensuring the security of Israel.”

Who is Naftali Bennett, the hard-charging, high-tech millionaire becoming Israel's next prime minister?

Real quick

Judge sides with hospital in first federal ruling on vaccine mandates

In the first federal ruling on vaccine mandates, a Houston judge Saturday dismissed a lawsuit by hospital employees who declined the COVID-19 vaccine — a decision that could have a ripple effect across the nation. The case involved Houston Methodist, which was the first hospital system in the country to require that all its employees get vaccinated. U.S. District Judge Lynn N. Hughes said federal law does not prevent employers from issuing that mandate. “We can now put this behind us and continue our focus on unparalleled safety, quality, service and innovation," Houston Methodist's CEO Marc Boom said after the ruling. The lawsuit was filed by 117 workers and led by Jennifer Bridges, a nurse at Houston Methodist's Baytown hospital who declined the vaccine because she considers it experimental and dangerous. The judge disagreed, writing: "This claim is false, and it is also irrelevant."

'Condition of employment': Hospitals in DC, across the nation follow Houston Methodist in requiring vaccination for workers.

Djokovic storms back to win French Open

Novak Djokovic dropped the first two sets of the French Open final to 22-year-old Stefanos Tsitsipas, but the top-seeded Serbian rallied off three consecutive set wins to capture his second Grand Slam of the year and the 19th of his storied career. He's now just one Grand Slam title behind the men's record shared by Roger Federer and Rafael Nadal. Djokovic has now won seven of the last 11 Grand Slam events. Coupled with his 2016 French Open title, he's now won each of the four major tournaments at least twice. The 34-year-old can now set his sights on Wimbledon in his quest to become the first men's player since Rod Laver in 1969 to win all four Grand Slam titles in the same calendar year.

Unseeded Barbora Krejcikova wins French Open to take her first Grand Slam.

Manhunts in Savannah, Chicago, Austin after weekend shooting rampages

A manhunt was underway Sunday for the gunman who opened fire outside a residence in Savannah, Georgia, killing one person and wounding seven in one of three mass shootings across the nation over the weekend. Chicago and Austin, Texas also saw shootings with multiple victims late Friday into early Saturday. Police in Chicago were searching for suspects after one woman was killed and nine others left injured by gunfire. In Austin, 14 people were injured when shots rang out in the city's Sixth Street entertainment district.

The U.S. saw a spike in shootings and homicides last year, and many experts cited pandemic-related stress amid a rise in gun ownership and debate over policing. The numbers appear to be remaining high. Nationwide, there have been 17 mass killings so far this year, defined as at least four people killed, not including the shooter, according to a USA TODAY/Associated Press/Northeastern University database. Sixteen of those were shootings.

P.S. Like this roundup of stories? Sign up for "The Short List" newsletter here.

This is a compilation of stories from across the USA TODAY Network. Contributing: Associated Press.

search_result.title: Former Gross talks about living and “dying” in the kitchen - USA Today
search_result.text:
By Wine & Food Experience

Dec. 19, 2018, 6:00 a.m. ET

Wrigley Mansion Executive Chef and 1995 James Beard recipient Christopher Gross discusses life in and out of the kitchen in this Q&A for USA TODAY’s Wine & Food Experience.

What’s your morning routine?

In my business, it’s hard to have a routine, but during the times that there is one, I have French press coffee with my girlfriend and a quick workout before I head to work.

What inspired you to be a chef?

I wasn’t fast enough to be a pro super bike or motor cross racer, so I went with Chef instead.

Who’s your mentor?

Michel Richard and Jean- Louis Palladin

Describe your formal training

I never had any formal training, I was just thrown into the kitchen and worked my way up. I learned technical French cooking in various kitchens in Paris and throughout France before returning home to the U.S. in my early twenties.

What do you get out of participating in the USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience?

I enjoy the camaraderie between chefs! Seeing old friends and meeting new friends are big reasons why I love to participate.

What do you cook at home?

Simple dishes, such as roasted chicken with root vegetables, are my favorites to cook when I’m at home. Recipes that require little hands-on effort are the best for a quiet night in.

What’s your favorite dish?

Roasted chicken, which is also why I make it at home!

What’s your favorite holiday dish?

My mom’s noodles, which are more like dumplings. It’s classic Midwestern comfort food (I’m from Missouri).

What was best day in the kitchen

Not losing my job in a kitchen in Paris after they found out I was not in Paris legally! That was a defining moment in my career!

Who is the coolest person you’ve cooked for?

Cooking for Oliver Stone and having drinks with him after

What’s your favorite food trend happening right now?

The food trend of going back to basics.

Tell us something you would like your followers to know about you.

I’ll be in the kitchen until I die.

How do you end your day?

A Laphroaig whisky with my girlfriend

What’s the weirdest food combination that you enjoy?

I love to put French fries or potato chips in my hamburger

What’s the next new concept percolating in your world? (What new plans do you have in store?)

I am working on a new restaurant (Christopher’s at the Wrigley Mansion) that should open in the fall of 2019! 26-seat tasting menu restaurant in a glass building. I also have a restaurant opening in the Phoenix Sky Harbor airport in January 2019 called Christopher’s at Sky Harbor.

What achievement are you most proud of (awards or recognition or other)?

The Food and Wine Best New Chefs recognition (in 1989) kicked off my career and then the James Beard Award (Best Chef Southwest) followed. However, being recognized by my peers is always the most gratifying.

What advice would you give to aspiring chefs?

Work hard, work in the best places you can, and don’t worry about money.

What advice would you give to cooks at home?

Don’t worry about following a savory recipe verbatim. You can add your own personal touch or substitute an ingredient. Taste, taste, taste, as you go!

To find out more about how you can attend and enjoy a wine and food experience, visit wineandfood.usatoday.com.

search_result.title: White with steak and leftover wine? David Lynch isn't afraid to break ...
search_result.text:
By Wine & Food Experience

April 15, 2019, 6:00 a.m. ET

SommSelect’s editorial director and two-time James Beard Award recipient, sommelier and author of critically acclaimed ‘Vino Italiano’ David Lynch shares his oenophile insights, love for his son, recollections about his mentors, how he chooses a good glass of wine - and what he felt on the evening he received his first James Beard Award with Daniel Boulud as presenter.

Lynch is a featured wine authority on the 10-city 2019 USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience tour.

What’s your morning routine?

These days it usually involves driving my son (12) to school. It’s the best unless he’s crabby: He asks lots and lots of questions I am increasingly unqualified to answer, and he also DJ’s as we drive.

What inspired you to be a wine expert/sommelier?

Both during and after college, I got to know Lidia Bastianich through her son, Joe, who was my classmate and friend. Visiting her home and experiencing Felidia Ristorante as a shaggy haired college kid was a revelation for me. I don’t know if I decided to be a sommelier then—my main goal coming out of college was to be a writer—but I knew there was a world of food and wine that I wanted to explore.

Who was/is your mentor?

I’ve had a lot of mentors over the years. I look at mentorship as interdisciplinary, in the sense that you can have both “personal” and “professional” mentors. My parents were big personal mentors—inspirations for how to be a good, honest person. Professionally, I’d certainly cite Joe and Lidia Bastianich and Mario Batali. But there have been many other personal and professional mentors along the way.

Describe your formal training.

My first real wine training came during my tenure at Wine & Spirits magazine in New York. Being able to sit in on the tasting panels with an assortment of top NYC sommeliers was invaluable, as was the sheer quantity of wine tasted. I also, before joining the magazine as an editor/writer, attended the legendary wine course at Windows on the World conducted by sommelier Kevin Zraly. I signed up as a “pourer” and served my fellow classmates before sitting down and participating myself. But aside from that, my training came on the job: When I wrote “Vino Italiano “ (Clarkson-Potter; 2002) I spent a year in Italy traveling and tasting and really diving deep into the Italian wine scene. After I returned from Italy in 2001 I dove right into being a sommelier at Babbo in NYC. It was a trial by fire that eventually culminated in me becoming the General Manager of the restaurant.

Describe your biggest challenge in becoming a “sommelier.”

Not to sound immodest but it wasn’t that challenging, other than balancing the administrative tasks with the service component, which at a busy place like Babbo was pretty intense. Long days for sure.

What achievement are you most proud of (awards or recognition or other)?

The publication of my two books: “Vino Italiano” (with Joe Bastianich) and “The Wine Snob’s Dictionary” (with David Kamp).

Describe the most memorable day in your career.

Probably the night I stood at the podium at the James Beard Awards and accepted the award for Outstanding Wine Service given to Babbo (2004). I was in a tuxedo, I got off a good joke, and generally felt amazing. Daniel Boulud was the presenter!

Who is the coolest person you’ve ever consulted for? [i.e., Celebrity? Titan of industry? Global chef?]

I worked for Mario Batali for seven years. He’s an incredibly talented, literate guy who, along with Joe Bastianich, captured lightning in a bottle with Babbo.

The era during which I was there (2001-2007) was chock-full of cool, influential people. It wasn’t simply a “celebrity” scene: It was people who shaped the culture.

Being able to occasionally talk to these people about wine was a very heady experience for me, because again, I’m not talking about Screech from “Saved by The Bell.”

I’m talking about Bill Clinton and Vernon Jordan; Bono and the U2 crew; Mike Nichols and Diane Sawyer; David Chase and a bunch of “Sopranos” people; Maggie and Jake Gyllenhaal; Michael Stipe; Jim Nelson of “GQ”; Geddy Lee from Rush (really knows his wine) and Billy Squier (same); Jay McInerney; Wes Anderson…really the list goes on and on. To me it epitomized New York City.

What do you look forward to accomplishing during the 2019 USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience tour?

Showing people that wine guys can be fun.

What is your go-to wine at home, simply kicking back and relaxing?

As I’ve gotten older I’ve gravitated toward more white wine and much lighter reds. Lately my go-to has been Cru Beaujolais (not Italian, I know), served at refrigerator temperature.

What’s your favorite wine and food pairing?

Barolo served with the Piedmontese pasta dish Agnolotti dal Plin.

While we’re on that, what’s the weirdest wine and food combo you secretly enjoy?

Crisp, mineral white wine (i.e. Chablis) and steak (not that weird actually: try it).

What tips and essential tools do you recommend to enjoy wine at its best?

Temperature, temperature, temperature! Don’t serve your whites too cold or your reds too warm! (50 degrees for whites, 60 for reds!) And don’t worry if you somehow have leftover wine: You don’t need to gas it or do anything to it if you plan on revisiting it the next day (it will likely be even better). Just cork it and put it in the fridge (yes, reds too).

What advice would you give the average person buying wine? What to look for? [For example: Star rating? Expert reviews? Where it’s from for no-fail quality? Pretty label? etc…]

Develop relationships with retailers and sommeliers. Do not let yourself believe a wine has to be expensive to be good. Be willing to go outside your comfort zone and try new varieties and regions.

Which region in the world is the next up and coming to produce great wines – one to watch?

Galicia, Spain is top-of-mind for me right now.

What is the leading wine trend happening right now?

The lightening of red wines. Consumers are beginning to respond to reds that are bright, unoaked, and refreshing—as opposed to inky and massive.

Tell us something you would like your followers to know about you.

I’d rather keep it mysterious, thanks.

What’s the next new concept percolating for you? (What new plans do you have in store?)

I hope to write another wine book someday. And maybe a novel.

How do you end your day?

With a snuggle with my son.

To find out more about how you can attend and enjoy a wine and food experience, visit wineandfood.usatoday.com.

search_result.title: Column: Going back home to celebrate failure - USA Today
search_result.text:
USATODAY

Updated Oct. 4, 2012, 5:40 p.m. ET

Thomas Wolfe wrote 'You Can't Go Home Again'. He was wrong.

I spent most of my adult years in New York City, Washington, D.C., and Florida.

I return to my home state of South Dakota to remember and observe successes or failures. T

SIOUX FALLS and VERMILLION, S.D. -- Best-selling author Thomas Wolfe's most famous fictional novel was entitled You Can't Go Home Again. He was wrong.

Wolfe was born and grew up in Asheville, N.C. He moved on to make his fame and fortune in New York City. He wrote seven novels and 32 short novels. His most famous was published after he died from tuberculous of the brain at age 37 in 1938.

You Can't Go Home Again was about a fictional hometown called Libya Hill. But Carolinians considered it a smear of his birth state. City slickers still often cite the book as the reason they don't go back to where they were born.

I spent most of my adult years in New York City, Washington, D.C., and Florida. I return to my home state of South Dakota to remember and observe successes or failures. This week I did both:

Honored Marilyn Hagerty, who electrified the culinary world with her classic review of an Olive Garden restaurant that opened in her hometown of Grand Forks, N.D. Hagerty followed in the footsteps of national media stars like Walter Cronkite, Tom Brokaw, Larry King, Bob Schieffer and Katie Couric as the latest recipient of the Al Neuharth Award at the University of South Dakota for a lifetime of excellence in the media.

Observe the 60th anniversary of the start of SoDak Sports, a statewide weekly tabloid that my college friend Bill Porter and I started in 1952. We went broke with it in two years. Despite that, the South Dakota Press Association and the Sioux Falls Area Chamber of Commerce sponsored a reception for journalistic and political guests at Sid's Liquor Store, where SoDak Sports rented a vacant room as its first office.

I've come back home every year since 1989 to present this award — and to enjoy the refreshing atmosphere of my native state.

Feedback:

"Lessons learned from his first failed publishing venture propelled Al Neuharth to become one of America's most successful newspapermen. South Dakotans are proud of our native son and fortunate he does return home often."

—David Bordewyk, general manager, S. D. Newspaper Association

"Revisiting failures without getting stuck in the past is a sign of winners. They have the confidence to learn from mistakes, knowing that they'll do better the next time."

—Rosabeth Moss Kanter, Harvard Business School professor, author of Confidence

"Mr. Neuharth, in very good humor and perspective as always, shared a quote related to his experience with SoDak Sports: 'You can learn more from failure than success if you're paying attention.' Wise words for all of us to follow. He has amply proven this throughout his career in journalism and by founding USA TODAY, a remarkable success in journalistic development."

—Evan Nolte, president/CEO, Sioux Falls Area Chamber of Commerce

search_result.title: She's the best female chef in North America, and she's right here in GA
search_result.text:
June 6, 2026, 5:05 a.m. ET

Chef Mashama Bailey, the culinary force behind The Grey in Savannah, has been named North America's Best Female Chef for 2026 by The World's 50 Best, an organization that recognizes leading restaurants and culinary professionals around the globe.

The award places Bailey among the continent's most influential figures in food and hospitality and marks another milestone in a career that has helped bring international attention to Savannah's dining scene.

Here's what to know.

Who is Mashama Bailey?

Bailey was born in the Bronx and followed a path that included working as a private chef in New York City before pursuing additional culinary training in France.

Her career gained national prominence after she partnered with entrepreneur John O. Morisano to open The Grey, a restaurant housed inside a restored former Greyhound bus station in Savannah.

Since opening, the restaurant has earned widespread recognition for its approach to Southern cuisine, blending traditional techniques and ingredients with contemporary influences while exploring the stories and history behind the food.

Why is The Grey so well known?

The Grey has become one of the most acclaimed restaurants in the South and has helped establish Savannah as a destination for food lovers from around the world.

The restaurant's location inside a renovated Jim Crow-era bus station has also drawn attention, serving as a backdrop for a dining experience that often explores themes of history, culture and identity through food.

Bailey's work at The Grey has earned her multiple honors throughout the years, including James Beard Awards in 2019 and 2022.

What other honors has Bailey received recently?

The latest recognition comes just weeks after Bailey was named to TIME's 2026 TIME100 list, which highlights some of the world's most influential people.

For more information, visit theworlds50best.com/stories.

Vanessa Johns is the Trending Topics Reporter for the Deep South Connect Team Georgia, covering food and entertainment. Email her at Vcountryman@gannett.com.

search_result.title: I traveled US to highlight food. What I found was community. | Opinion
search_result.text:
Lidia Bastianich

Opinion contributor

Nov. 30, 2025, 6:01 a.m. ET

When I came to the United States as a young refugee, my family had very little – except for the kindness of the neighbors who welcomed us. They brought us food, helped my mother find work and reminded us, in a new and uncertain world, that we were not alone. Those early experiences shaped my life and my cooking. And memories of them came rushing back as I traveled across the country this year filming my new PBS special.

In every place I visited – from wildfire-scarred neighborhoods outside Los Angeles to a pay-what-you-can café in Denver – I met ordinary Americans doing something extraordinary: stepping in for one another at a time when food prices, rents and daily anxieties are rising faster than many families can keep up.

Indeed, food insecurity in America is increasing. I saw the strain in every community I visited. When Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program benefits were temporarily halted recently, nearly 42 million Americans felt it – food pantries saw lines lengthen and parents told me they were stretching every ingredient they had.

While SNAP benefits have now been reinstated, the need has not disappeared. For many families, the safety net is still too thin, and new restrictions could put millions more at risk of losing the support that helps them feed their children with dignity.

As a result, people across the country are improvising their own safety nets. In Colorado, a mother cooks extra lasagna because she knows the family down the hall is struggling. In California, neighbors organize a burrito night so that no one on the block goes to bed hungry.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

As one recent news story highlighted, this grassroots generosity is growing as food prices soar and paychecks fall short. These small acts aren’t simply about food – they are about dignity. They say, I see you. You matter. You belong.

'Lidia Celebrates America' explores food as a tool to rebuild community

That belief is at the heart of my PBS special, "Lidia Celebrates America: A Nation of Neighbors."

At SAME Café in Denver, a guest can pay with money, vegetables from their garden or even an hour of volunteer time. No questions asked. At District 10 Community Market in San Francisco – the first legislatively supported free grocery store in the country – families choose their ingredients just as they would in any supermarket, surrounded not by red tape but by respect. In Los Angeles, I watched chefs and volunteers cook hot meals for families who had lost everything in the January wildfires, with pots on the stove, music in the air, hope simmering beside grief.

What I witnessed confirmed something I have believed my whole life: Food is one of the most powerful tools we have for rebuilding community.

When prices rise and budgets collapse, we can still cook for the sick neighbor, drop off a meal for the single parent working two shifts or share what we grow, bake or buy. We can still pull up another chair. We can still treat each other as human beings worthy of simple, everyday grace.

Growing up in a tight-knit community that knew hardship well, my grandmother would say, “If you have a loaf of bread, you always have enough to share.” That wisdom followed me from a refugee camp to my first restaurant kitchen and into every home I’ve visited across America.

Yes, policymakers must address the economic pressures squeezing families – by strengthening the programs that keep food on the table during hard times, by supporting the small farmers and grocers who keep our communities fed, and by making sure that no child goes to school hungry.

'A Nation of Neighbors' shows the America I know. We can strengthen it.

These are long-term solutions that require commitment, not just during a crisis, but every day. Even the best policies, however, cannot replace what happens when one neighbor shows up for another. While leaders work on systemic change, the work of caring for each other begins at our own kitchen tables.

This generosity is a reminder of the America we aspire to be. And today, we need that reminder more than ever.

Every time we offer a meal – no matter how simple – we communicate something much deeper: that we are tied together, that our fates are connected, and that a nation is strongest not when everyone has the same, but when everyone has someone.

I invite you to look around your neighborhood. Be intentional about getting to know everyone living around you. Who could use a warm dish this week? Who might need a friendly knock on the door? You don’t need to run a café or a free market. You simply need to care.

In "A Nation of Neighbors," I met countless people who are not waiting for permission, funding or the “right moment” to help. They saw a need and they cooked. They organized. They welcomed. They created community from scratch.

This is the America I know. This is the America that welcomed me. And this is the America we can strengthen – one meal, one gesture, one neighbor at a time. Let’s build on this movement.

Lidia Bastianich is an award-winning public television host, chef, author and restaurateur. Her new national special, "Lidia Celebrates America: A Nation of Neighbors," premiered Nov. 25 and is continuing to air on PBS.

search_result.title: Chef James Rigato talks about mentors, his daily routine and the ...
search_result.text:
Wine & Food Experience

Feb. 1, 2019, 3:00 a.m. ET

Chef James Rigato talks about mentors, his daily routine and the frequent culinary collaborations with fellow chefs at his famed Detroit-area dining spot, Mabel Gray. He dreamed up the concept for gourmet improvisation during downtime filming Top Chef. (Alas, he didn’t emerge the victor, but Mabel Gray is a hit!) The James Beard Award semifinalist is a featured chef at the Detroit Free Press Wine & Food Experience, a stop on the USA TODAY culinary tour.

Q&A with James Rigato

What’s your morning routine?

I wake up sometime between 7 and 830 am. I need coffee within 5 minutes of waking up and my Jura coffee machine with Water Street Coffee from Kalamazoo is one of my better investments. I have 2 dogs, Oda Mae the Doberman and Chandler, the pit bull. So, feeding them, letting them out, snuggling, kisses and playtime is one of my first obligations of the day.

I’ve been working on health and fitness for the last year or so, and a trip to the gym is common 3-4 times a week. I like private training, spin and the occasional boot camp. I never thought I’d say this but working out helps me with long days and the physical demand of being a chef/owner. I’m walking in the door at Mabel Gray, my restaurant, anywhere between 8 and 10 a.m. I like to be the first one in, and be alone with prep, creating new menu ideas and listening to music for the first few hours of the day.

What inspired you to be a chef?

I started as a dishwasher when I was 14. The underground society of restaurant workers combined with the freedom, constant stimulation and camaraderie drew me in quickly. Being able to learn on the job from talented individuals of all walks of life kept me pursuing goals that I once thought out of reach. Over the years, the gratification, creative freedom and travel have been incredible incentives to work harder and continue educating myself and pursuing self-improvement.

Who’s your mentor?

I’ve been fortunate to have many mentors in different corners of the industry. But the most frequent influence in my life and career would be Luciano Del Signore of Bacco Ristorante, Bigalora and Farm + Ferment group. As a chef, entrepreneur and Italian American, Luciano is a constant source of knowledge and leadership for me. We’re a great team.

Describe your formal training

I graduated from Schoolcraft Culinary Arts Program in 2005.

What do you get out of participating in the USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience?

The USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience is a fantastic event to be a part of. I enjoy the interaction with guests who are celebrating the food and beverage culture of the city. It’s always a good time to see my fellow chefs and restaurant friends hanging out and working together. And any event that’s good enough for Martha Stewart is good enough for me!

What do you cook at home?

Because my restaurant, Mabel Gray, is so close to my house I rarely cook at home. But when I do its usually pasta. I love clams puttanesca or midnight linguine with a nice bottle of wine and crusty bread. Eating it right out of the pan avoiding the dogs trying to steal a bite sounds accurate.

What’s your favorite dish?

I really can’t say. We handwrite the a la carte menu daily and the tasting menu is ever changing, even in the middle of service. So, I wouldn’t say I have a signature dish or something I’ve been working on my whole life. I like to change directions on the menu like tempos change in jazz. Also, with the weather in Michigan, one day you’re reaching for something raw and refreshing and the next you’re braising shanks to fight off the foot of snow.

What’s your favorite holiday dish?

Christmas ham. This year we ran a griddled Berkshire ham dish with Madeira, ham bone stock, button mushrooms, charred onion and clove butter inspired by the classic Christmas ham. Timeless flavors.

What was the best day in the kitchen

Mabel Gray was designed to be a collaborative space. No permanent menu, no walk-in cooler, a large stainless countertop in the middle of the room where we cook and plate daily. This intentional concept and design allows many guest chefs to come in, collaborate, takeover and experiment. Hosting guest chefs and winemakers is what inspires me here. Every collaboration becomes my favorite day in the kitchen. Our wild cider dinners with Ryan Burk, head cider maker at Angry Orchard, are always incredibly gratifying experiences.

Who is the coolest person you’ve cooked for?

This is a tie. Yo-Yo Ma is honestly the coolest guy you’d ever want to meet let alone cook for. We did a private dinner at the Detroit Symphony Orchestra and he was so gracious, funny and genuinely interested in us. Even insisted on a squad photo with us. Love that guy.

The other one would be Amos Lee. Amos and I met through a mutual friend and keep in touch while he’s on the road eating at some of my recommendations and friends’ restaurants. When he was in town last, I couldn’t make it to his concert, so I sent my girlfriend and her sister instead.

Amos jumped in my girlfriend’s car after the show and brought his crew up to Mabel for an after-hours dinner. After a few drinks, he surprised us with his guitar and a private little show because I couldn’t make it to his concert. It was a very special night and a stone-cold G move on his part. Both those guys are as good as it gets.

What’s your favorite food trend happening right now?

Veganism. I really enjoy cooking and eating vegan.

Tell us something you would like your followers to know about you.

I binge watch old Unsolved Mysteries episodes and then have anxious fever dreams about them.

How do you end your day?

I go home, shower and snuggle with my pups and girlfriend. If I’m awake 30 minutes after being home, it’s a miracle.

What’s the weirdest food combination that you enjoy?

Ketchup and mustard on my chili fries. Very controversial around these parts.

What’s the next new concept percolating in your world? (What new plans do you have in store?)

I don’t kiss and tell.

What achievement are you most proud of (awards or recognition or other)?

I’m most proud of the team that I’ve built here at Mabel. All recognition we receive reflects their dedication and hard work.

What advice would you give to aspiring chefs?

Don’t rely on the workday to take you to the next level. Read, take notes, reflect, stage, travel, eat, learn about wine and spirits, study management techniques and business. No one in the business really wants you to succeed. It’s up to you and you alone.

What advice would you give to cooks at home?

Don’t be scared. Taste, think, adapt and overcome.

To find out more about how you can attend and enjoy a wine and food experience, visit wineandfood.usatoday.com.

search_result.title: Vonn, Smith, Walsh Jennings join West Michigan High School ...
search_result.text:
The Holland Sentinel

April 12, 2021, 6:01 a.m. ET

Olympic gold medalist and four-time World Cup skiing champion Lindsey Vonn, 2020 NFL Comeback Player of the Year Alex Smith, and three-time volleyball Olympic gold medalist Kerri Walsh Jennings have joined the star-studded cast of presenters and guests for the West Michigan High School Sports Awards — which will also include a virtual red carpet with Matt James and Tyler Cameron of “The Bachelor.”

Top professional athletes will help recognize west Michigan area student-athletes by announcing player of the year award winners. The show includes honorees, finalists and winners in more than 20 sports and will also recognize non-sporting excellence in a variety of categories. The awards program culminates with some of the biggest names in professional sports announcing the Coach of the Year, Team of the Year, and the student-athletes who will take the top honors of Male and Female Athlete of the Year.

Smith will be announcing this year’s recipient of the Courage Award, which honors student-athletes who have shown great courage and inspired those around them when faced with challenges and obstacles in their life, either on or off the field.

In his first season as the starting quarterback for the Washington Football Team, Smith suffered a severe injury that almost resulted in the amputation of his right leg. After numerous surgeries, months in the hospital and then two long stints of rehab, Smith returned to the field. He didn’t miss a beat, re-claiming the role of starting quarterback and leading Washington to the NFC East championship. His resilience and determination made him the runaway favorite to win the NFL’s 2020 Comeback Player of the Year Award.

Former World Cup alpine ski racer for the U.S. Ski Team, Vonn is one of only two female skiers to win four World Cup overall championships. She claims the second highest super ranking of all skiers — men or women —and earned a record eight World Cup season titles in the downhill discipline. Her long list of medals and championships makes Vonn the most successful American ski racer, and she is considered one of the greatest skiers in the world.

Also known as “Six Feet of Sunshine,” Walsh Jennings, 42, will announce the winners for volleyball. She’s looking to compete alongside partner Brooke Sweat in the 2021 Tokyo Olympic Games. She became the most decorated beach volleyball Olympian of all time after winning bronze in 2016. This five-time Olympian has three gold and one bronze medal in beach volleyball and holds the record for tournament victories with 135 for her career.

James and Cameron both attended Wake Forest University and played football for the Atlantic Coast Conference Division 1 team. They are American television personalities, entrepreneurs and activists. In 2019, Cameron was cast in Season 15 of “The Bachelorette.” Soon after, James was cast in his historical role as the first Black male lead on ABC’s hit show, “The Bachelor.” They are also co-founders of ABC Food Tours, a non-profit organization whose mission is to empower students by providing experiences across diverse cultures and career opportunities.

The West Michigan High School Sports Awards is a multi-month student-athlete recognition program that culminates in a year-end awards show June 30. Due to COVID-19 safety precautions for students and guest speakers, the event was converted to an on-demand broadcast show for 2021. Honoree selections for state sanctioned sports, who are all featured in the show, are announced throughout the year on the event website.

The show will be free to watch on any smartphone or computer thanks to local business sponsors. Honorees should register on the show website to reserve their complimentary athlete gift box. Registrations are also used to send out awards for winners following the show.

The most elite high school athletes, coaches and teams from state-sanctioned sports across the U.S. will be featured in the inaugural USA TODAY High School Sports Awards. Statewide athlete of the year winners will be among the national honorees.

search_result.title: These top restaurants in Colorado have earned a Michelin Guide star
search_result.text:
Sept. 13, 2023, 6:46 p.m. ET

Five restaurants in Colorado received Michelin Guide stars for the first time earlier this week. This award is considered one of the highest accolades in the culinary industry.

Colorado is the sixth region in the U.S. to receive a Michelin Guide. California, Illinois, New York, Florida, and Washington, D.C. also have Michelin Guides. It was announced in June that the Michelin Guide was coming to Colorado.

The following Colorado restaurants earned Michelin Guide stars:

Beckon , Denver (Duncan Holmes)

, Denver (Duncan Holmes) Bosq , Aspen (Barclay Dodge)

, Aspen (Barclay Dodge) Brutø , Denver (Michael Diaz de Leon and Kelly Whitaker)

, Denver (Michael Diaz de Leon and Kelly Whitaker) Frasca Food and Wine , Boulder (Ian Palazzola)

, Boulder (Ian Palazzola) The Wolf’s Tailor, Denver (Taylor Stark and Kelly Whitaker)

What does the Michelin star mean for restaurants?

Michelin Guide's International Director Gwendal Poullennec talked to USA TODAY about the meaning of the award.

"Receiving a Michelin Guide distinction is a career milestone for restaurants and their teams. It’s an honor that many chefs have worked for years to achieve," Poullennec shared.

"But it’s also becoming part of a family, made up of the most talented culinary professionals in the world. Lives change almost overnight. Restaurants included in the Michelin Guide see increased attention from foodies, and reservations become highly sought-after."

What is the Michelin Green Star?

Michelin introduced the Green Star in 2020. This is an award for restaurants that show an outstanding commitment to environmental sustainability. The following Colorado restaurants received the distinction:

Blackbelly Market, Boulder

Boulder Bramble & Hare, Boulder

Boulder Brutø, Denver

Denver The Wolf’s Tailor, Denver

What is a Michelin star?: Here's what to expect at a restaurant with the badge of honor.

Bougie on a budget: These are the cheapest Michelin-starred restaurants in the world

When will the next awards be announced?

The Michelin Guide announced it will award stars at a ceremony on Nov. 7 in New York City. The organization will award stars to restaurants in New York, Chicago, and Washington, D.C. at the same time.

What cities does the Michelin Guide include?

The Michelin Guide has grown a considerable amount in the last century. While the publication started off just in France, there are now 64 guide selections worldwide.

When did the Michelin Guide start?

The Michelin Guide had its start in the late 19th century, according to its website.

In France, in 1889, Edouard and Andre Michelin launched their tire company, Michelin. Seeking to help their customer base plan trips — in hopes of boosting the sales of automobiles and tires — they developed a guide that listed places to dine and stay overnight along with where to get fuel and how to change a tire. In 1920, they published their first Michelin Guide that cost money. It was sold for seven francs.

The Michelin Guide arrived in the U.S. in November 2005 with a New York edition released in 2006. The first issuance included 39 stars. Famous chefs Eric Ripert’s Le Bernardin and Thomas Keller's Per Se both earned 3-stars.

Morgan Hines contributed to this reporting

search_result.title: USA TODAY Wine & Food Experience asks Chef Scott Conant 18 ...
search_result.text:
By Wine & Food Experience

Dec. 11, 2018, 6:00 a.m. ET

Wine & Food Experience sits down with Award-winning chef, cookbook author and television personality Chef Scott Conant. The well-rounded and well-respected chef, whose television credits include Top Chef, Anthony Bourdain: No Reservations and Beat Bobby Flay, was also the Season 3 winner of Chopped All-Starts on the Food Network. His sought after Italian cuisine has earned him recognition - evident in his many accolades – which includes the Best New Restaurant of 2003 James Beard Foundation award for his restaurant L’impero. His three cookbooks are highly-rated for offering recipes for delicious Italian fare.

1. What’s your morning routine?

The first thing I do every morning – before I wake up my daughters – is make a triple espresso for myself. Triple espresso over ice, and then I wake my daughters up. I get ready, get them ready for school, and drop them off and go directly to the gym. Every morning, if I’m home, that’s what I do.

2. What inspired you to be a chef?

Two things happened simultaneously that got me into the kitchen. No. 1, I was going to high school and got a job as a dishwasher in my family friend’s restaurant. I loved it. At the same time and completely unrelated, I went to a vocational school for high school. You get to choose your trade, any number of things – it could be auto-mechanics, carpentry, etc. I chose plumbing and I couldn’t get into the program because too many people had applied. So as a second choice, I chose culinary arts. That combination of working in a restaurant and being enrolled in culinary arts — I jumped right into being a cook. Very quickly, after washing dishes for a couple of months, I started cooking and prepping and working my way up in the kitchen while attending school for the next four years. I kind of fell into being a chef, put on the path because I couldn’t get into plumbing, but cooking became the natural progression.

3. Who’s my mentor?

Over the years, I’ve worked with wonderful people who, unselfishly, helped steer me in the right direction or assist me in business. I cannot say that I’ve had one mentor, but I’ve always found myself inspired by the good qualities of characters in books that I’ve read, and I try to draw upon those traits to guide me, help me grow, and put myself in a position where I can treat my team the way they deserve to be treated.

4. Describe your formal training.

I went to vocational school for culinary arts and then later attend the Culinary Institute of America in Hyde Park, New York. I later went to Europe and traveled on and off for a few years. Then I worked in several restaurants, honing my skills, and took a leap of faith in my mid-20s to being a chef. I constantly continue to educate myself, whether it’s in books or online.

5. What do you get out of participating in the Wine & Food Experience?

First of all, I love the interaction with the guests. It is such a good time and we have so much fun. I love to tell stories from the kitchen and about my colleagues. Their reactions and taking photos together really makes it all worth it. I love learning from everyone as well as teaching. Nothing better than that.

6. What do you cook at home?

I love to cook for my daughters. I like to feel inspired so it depends. A couple of days ago, I made a huge plate of fresh pasta with white truffles. My 8-year-old, Ayla, was just over the moon with eating the truffles. I never ate a truffle until well into my 20’s and to see my 8-year-old scarfing down a bowl, it was heartwarming. My 6-year-old won’t have it. She doesn’t want anything to do with them; she doesn’t like the way they look and she likes the scent even less. That’s okay, I’ll have her share.

7. What’s your favorite dish?

I can eat my mother’s chicken cutlets by the backpack, I always say. I just love those things. And also her sausage and peppers. I loved it when my mother made that when I was a kid.

8. What’s your favorite holiday dish?

I’ve always craved Thanksgiving — it’s one of my favorite holidays, especially now that I’m a father — I love to have people over to the house. I don’t think there’s necessarily one favorite food item, but it’s really more about the energy of the family being together. Reese’s Peanut Butter Cups on Halloween — that’s probably my favorite.

9. What was your best day in the kitchen?

It’s hard to say. I’ve been doing this for a pretty long time and I love cooking for people. It was probably one time I cooked for my parents. My father had a pretty discerning palette, he didn’t like a lot of foods. You know, now that he’s not around anymore, I think those days of cooking for him and that one dinner I made for him — I think it was like 18 courses at one of my old restaurants — he ate everything and was really happy. That was special.

10. Coolest person you’ve ever cooked for?

Miles Davis and Pelé were regulars at my old restaurants, and they are definitely top two cool people. I also cooked at Willie Nelson’s ranch a few years ago and that was awesome.

11. What’s your favorite food trend happening right now?

I’ll be honest with you, I’m not a huge “trend” guy. I do like to eat healthy as often as possible. I see a lot of healthy restaurants opening up all over the place so I feel like that’s something I can get behind — because it allows me to both eat healthy and check out new spots.

12. Tell us something you’d like your follows to know about you.

I feel like I have a certain reputation by doing TV shows. I think what people don’t realize through the edits, I like to think that I’m actually a lot of fun and love to laugh. I have a lingering reputation of being too serious.

13. How do you end your day?

I’m obsessed with politics and the news. I always end the day by either listening to POTUS radio on Sirius XM or catching up on the news on TV.

14. What’s the weirdest food combination that you enjoy?

When I was a kid, I used to love mortadella and Oreo cookies together. Sounds repulsive today but I used to love it; two very thinly sliced pieces of mortadella, I’d wrap it around the Oreo and eat it!

15. What’s the newest concept percolating in your world?

I recently launched a new product line called Sprezza Foods. I have a tomato sauce that I’ve been cooking for years and we’ve tweaked and adjusted the recipe and finally jarred it. It’s convenient for when you just don’t have the time to make the sauce completely from scratch or when tomatoes aren’t in season. The Sprezza collection features Italian sauces, spreads and pantry essentials. It’s fantastic and I’m so excited about it.

16. What achievement are you most proud of?

I’m proud of the James Beard awards and New York Times 3-stars and all that kind of stuff, opening restaurants in different markets and writing cookbooks, but I really think there’s something to be said when your kids are proud of their dad. That is just so special to me and I get all weepy. That’s the best achievement.

17. What advice would you give to aspiring chefs?

I wish that someone took me aside when I was younger, and said, “I know you think you’re on the right path, I know you think you’re smarter than everyone else” but it’s one thing to work hard, and another thing to work smart. I wish that I hadn’t gone out drinking and acting like an idiot when I was younger. I really wish I had taken classes before and after work, woken up early and pushed myself for further education instead of just spending 18 hours in the kitchen. I could’ve done more.

18. What advice can you give cooks at home?

Keep yourself organized. Lay out all of the ingredients you need first and then tackle the cooking process instead of running around doing too much. It really speaks volumes when you can build your layers of flavor the right way. It makes life much easier and ultimately you can enjoy the process.

For more information about the Martha Stewart Wine & Food Experience presented by USA TODAY, visit wineandfood.usatoday.com.

search_result.title: Des Moines festivals voted among the best in US for art, culture
search_result.text:
March 11, 2026, 12:14 p.m. ET

Des Moines recently made the USA TODAY'S 10BEST Readers' Choice Awards' list as one of the premier cities for art and cultural festivals.

Iowa's capital city is home to dozens of festivals every year, from music and food festivals to arts and entertainment. So out of all the festivals in Des Moines, which are some of the best in the country? Here's what to know.

What Des Moines festivals are among the best in the country, according to USA TODAY 10BEST?

The Des Moines Arts Festival and the World Food and Music Festival each placed in the Top 10 of their respective categories.

The Des Moines Arts Festival placed 10th on for best arts festivals, while the World Food and Music Festival was rated the eighth-best cultural festival in the U.S.

Here are the best art festivals in the U.S., according to USA TODAY 10BEST:

Orange Beach Festival of Art (Orange Beach, Alabama) Rio Grande Arts and Crafts Festival Balloon Festival Show (Albuquerque, New Mexico) International Festival of Arts & Ideas (New Haven, Connecticut) Artisphere (Greenville, South Carolina) Columbus Arts Festival (Columbus, Ohio) Festival of Arts & Pageant of the Masters (Laguna Beach, California) Old Town Art Fair (Chicago) Park City Kimball Arts Festival (Park City, Utah) The Woodlands Waterway Arts Festival (The Woodlands, Texas) Des Moines Arts Festival (Des Moines)

Here are the best cultural festivals in the U.S., according to USA TODAY 10BEST:

Fiestas de la Calle San Sebastián (San Juan, Puerto Rico) Black Wall Street Rally (Tulsa, Oklahoma) Dublin Irish Festival (Dublin, Ohio) Fyr Bal Festival (Ephraim, Wisconsin) Anchorage Fur Rendezvous (Anchorage, Alaska) Black Food Truck Festival (Charleston, South Carolina) Rougarou Fest (Houma, Louisiana) World Food and Music Festival (Des Moines) Celebrate Nashville Cultural Festival (Nashville) Celtic Music and Heritage Festival (St. Augustine, Florida)

In 2025, the Des Moines Arts Festival ranked No. 7 in the same USA TODAY 10Best cultural festival category, and the World Food and Music Festival placed fifth in the best city food festival.

What is the Des Moines Art Festival?

The annual multiday event dates back to the late 1950s and attracts hundreds of thousands of people every year to Western Gateway Park for musical performances, food and beverage stands, art exhibits and more.

The 2026 festival takes place from Friday, June 26, to Sunday, June 28.

What is Des Moines' World Food and Music Festival?

What started as a two-dozen-vendor event in the Historic East Village in 2005 has grown into a festival that draws roughly 100,000 people to the streets of downtown to experience one of Iowa's most vibrant international celebrations.

The festival typically takes place in August and showcases food and beverages from more than 30 countries, all alongside a three‑day lineup of music and performances from around the world.

The World Food and Music Festival runs from Friday, Aug. 28, to Sunday, Aug. 30, in 2026.

Cooper Worth is a service/trending reporter for the Des Moines Register. Reach him at cworth@gannett.com or follow him on X @CooperAWorth.

search_result.title: Trump's milestone birthday and what happens to your body at 80
search_result.text:
Updated June 14, 2026, 3:20 p.m. ET

President Donald Trump hit a milestone birthday today: June 14 marks the first day of his 80s. His predecessor, former President Joe Biden, also turned 80 while in office.

Throughout his second term, Trump – the oldest person ever elected president – has faced scrutiny about his health. His swollen legs, bruising on his hands and his perceived drowsiness – such as seemingly dozing off during the NBA finals on June 8 – have spurred reactions from everyday Americans and media members alike.

Ahead of the 2024 election, Biden prompted a national conversation around aging and leadership, and Trump has memorably called him “Sleepy Joe,” despite being just four years younger than Biden.

So as Trump enters this milestone decade, it begs the question – politics aside – what happens to your body in your 80s?

Experts agree that "80 isn’t what it used to be." When it comes to aging gracefully, factors like heart health, bone density, blood pressure and memory all play important roles in your 80s.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

'What kind of 80-year-old are you?'

The average life expectancy for men is 76.5 years, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). However, about half of men now live into their 80s, and those who are wealthier and more highly educated tend to live the longest, according to Steven Austad, a professor at the University of Alabama at Birmingham who is an expert on aging. For women, the average life expectancy sits above men at 81.4 years old.

“80 isn’t what it used to be,” Austad said. The question he asks instead is, “What kind of 80-year-old are you?”

Genetic, social and environmental factors all contribute to aging trajectories. Some 80-year-olds are frail and vulnerable to illness and injury, while others remain strong and active, according to researchers at Johns Hopkins Medicine.

While aging may not look the same for everyone, staying prepared and taking small steps to protect your health can go a long way. Here are three things you can expect to notice in your 80s.

Cognitive abilities may weaken as people age

On average, the brain shrinks in volume and weight about 5% every decade after we reach 40, and this process speeds up after 70, according to Harvard Health. This reduces the organ’s ability to communicate, so cognitive tasks can take longer.

Even people who age "normally,” without further complications, will struggle with remembering words, names, titles of movies and other trivial but routine recollections.

However, according to an August 2023 study in The Lancet Healthy Longevity, “super-agers” – adults older than 80 with the memory capacity of 50-year-olds – have larger brain volumes in areas linked to memory and movement, and their brain shrinkage is slower than the typical older adult.

While not all memory loss is reversible or preventable, there are tools to maintain and enhance cognitive function. These include staying physically active, eating a healthy diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids, managing stress, getting restful sleep, staying socially connected and doing activities that challenge your brain, such as puzzles, according to Michael Yassa, director of the Center for the Neurobiology of Learning and Memory at the University of California, Irvine.

"Being around people, engaging in stimulating activities and maintaining a large social network that's physical, not virtual, has been linked to better outcomes,” Yassa previously told USA TODAY.

Heart and blood vessels may become stiffer

Your heart performs the essential task of pumping blood to your body, but it’s common for arteries to become stiffer over time, according to the Mayo Clinic and the National Institute on Aging.

As you get older, your heart has to work harder to pump blood. It can’t beat as fast during physical activity or times of stress, which raises the risk of heart disease, heart failure and high blood pressure and can lead to a heart attack, cardiac arrest or stroke.

To mitigate heart health decline, it’s important to stay active. Mayo Clinic recommends completing at least 150 minutes of physical activity per week, whether that’s walking, swimming, dancing or any form of movement you enjoy. Eating a healthy diet with vegetables, fruits, whole grains, high-fiber foods and lean sources of protein, and avoiding saturated fats, added sugar and high levels of sodium, can also promote heart health.

Bone health and fractures in your 80s

Bone density peaks in your mid- to late 20s, and as we age, bones tend to weaken as they shrink in size and density, according to Johns Hopkins Medicine and the Mayo Clinic. Muscles can lose strength, endurance and flexibility, interfering with coordination, stability and balance, and making ol-der adults more susceptible to falls and bone fractures.

To help bones, joints and muscles stay healthy, it's crucial to get enough calcium and vitamin D.

Adults should aim to get at least 1,000 milligrams (mg) of calcium a day. Women 51 and older, and men 71 and older, should aim to get 1,200 mg a day. Consuming foods such as dairy products, broccoli, kale, salmon and tofu can increase your calcium intake without supplements.

Adults up to age 70 should aim to get 600 international units (IU) of vitamin D a day. Starting at age 70, this daily recommendation increases to 700 IU. Foods like tuna, trout, salmon and eggs are good sources of vitamin D.

Getting outside for a walk or jog can kill two birds with one stone − physical activity can support bone health, and the body naturally produces vitamin D when exposed to direct sunlight.

Contributing: Zac Anderson, Daryl Austin

search_result.title: Trump's big birthday plans. Dinner, peace talks and a UFC event
search_result.text:
Updated June 14, 2026, 8:03 p.m. ET

A family dinner, calls from well-wishers, an evening show – President Donald Trump’s 80th birthday is in some ways typical for a milestone celebration, with a few big twists.

Trump also spent his first day as an octogenarian Sunday, June 14 working to land a peace deal with Iran, and his after-dinner entertainment of a mixed martial arts cage match is one of the more unusual events in White House history.

Trump is just the second president to reach 80 in office, and his birthday comes amid questions about his health. For a president who has long sought to project strength, a birthday highlighting his advanced age isn’t something he has been eager to dwell on.

"You don't have to wish me happy birthday because I'm not happy about that birthday that I'm having," Trump told Dr. Mehmet Oz, administrator for Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, recently in the Oval Office. "That's a number that I never thought really too much about. It's not a number I like, but I'm here, nevertheless."

The birthday wishes poured in anyway. Top Trump administration officials and Republican lawmakers took to social media to congratulate the president. Russian President Vladmir Putin and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy both talked to Trump on his birthday.

'Winning at all costs': Trump wraps himself in UFC’s bareknuckle image

The calls were a reminder of Trump’s failed efforts to bring the Ukraine war to an end, but the president was focused on a different conflict Sunday that also has proven tricky to wind down.

Trump’s birthday began with a new round of Israeli military strikes in Beirut, which the country said was in response to projectiles fired by the militant group Hezbollah at Israel.

Israel’s escalation in Lebanon threated to derail Trump’s effort to reach a peace deal with Tehran, and the president took to social media to condemn the strikes.

"This could be the beginning of a long and beautiful peace — Let’s not blow it!" Trump said.

Trump and Iranian officials later announced that a deal had been reached, with an official signing ceremony planned for later in the week.

The push to get a peace deal finalized Sunday came as Trump was planning to celebrate his birthday later in the evening with a family dinner at the White House before taking in an Ultimate Fighting Championship event.

The South Lawn of the White House has been transformed into a makeshift UFC arena, including seating for thousands and an octagon fighting ring with a roughly 90-foot-tall "claw" built over it.

Trump has been hyping the event for months, bringing fighters into the Oval Office and displaying the UFC championship belt on his desk.

"These are real warriors," Trump said of the UFC fighters during a May 6 White House event. "When we talk about warriors, these are warriors."

Trump’s own fitness has been in the spotlight lately amid scrutiny of his health, including bruising on his hands, swelling in his legs and a habit of closing his eyes for stretches during public events, making it appear to some he was dozing.

Trump and his doctors say he’s in good shape, though. Capt. Sean Barbabella, the White House physician, wrote in summarizing the results of last month's physical that Trump "is fully fit to carry out all duties of the Commander-in-Chief and Head of State."

"I feel the same as I felt 50 years ago," Trump said May 4 at a White House event. "I'm not a senior. I'm far younger than a senior."
